---
title: |
  Penalized for Faith: Who Drives the Muslim Penalty?
subtitle: |
  Treatment-Effect Heterogeneity in European Conjoint Behavioral Games
abstract: |
  We reanalyize data
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  eval: true
  include: true
draft: true
draft-mode: visible
---

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Numerous studies have documented a significant ‘Muslim penalty’ across European societies [@helbling2014opposing; @helbling2012islamophobia].
In labour markets, housing, and everyday social interaction, individuals identified as Muslim are often disadvantaged relative to otherwise comparable non-Muslims.
Yet establishing the existence of this penalty, i.e. the systematic disadvantage incurred by individuals identified as Muslim, is only the starting point for a substantive understanding of the phenomenon.
The more demanding questions concern who discriminates, against whom, and under which conditions.

Against this background, this paper revisits a large-scale conjoint survey experiment conducted across 25 European Union member states by @hahm2024divided.
Using a combination of dictator and trust games alongside a conjoint design, they estimate the relative strength of multiple social divides.
While their main focus is on the dominance of the partisan divide, their analysis also identifies a notable anti-Muslim bias that is consistent across all 25 countries.
Whenever Muslim identity is experimentally signalled, respondents’ allocation decisions reveal a systematic penalty relative to otherwise comparable non-Muslim profiles.
What this finding does not reveal, however, is whether and how the magnitude of this penalty varies with the other attributes attached to Muslim profiles, with the characteristics and political orientations of respondents, or with the interaction between profiles and respondents.

This paper takes that heterogeneity as its central object of inquiry.
Treating the Muslim penalty as the primary quantity of interest, it shifts from the AMCE framework towards a Conditional Average Treatment Effect logic and distinguishes two sources of variation [@robinson2024detect].
The first concerns whether the remaining attributes of the displayed Muslim profile, such as class, nationality, or EU stance, moderate the magnitude of the penalty.
The second, and substantively more consequential, concerns whether individual-level characteristics of respondents, in particular their populist predispositions and their orientations towards European integration, account for who discriminates more strongly.
That the penalty is unlikely to constitute a single, undifferentiated response is suggested both by evidence that hostility towards Muslims is driven less by nominal religious affiliation than by perceived religiosity [@helbling2020islamophobia; @helbling2022muslim] and by the contextual, social-identity logic underlying the original study [@hahm2024divided].



In a broader context, the study promotes a more nuanced understanding of anti-Muslim discrimination in Europe.
By situating the variation in penalties within the cosmopolitan–populist divide that defines contemporary European politics, it suggests that the bias is less an isolated individual disposition than a politically driven phenomenon.
Understanding the conditions that amplify this bias is crucial for grasping the mechanisms that sustain it and the potential avenues for its mitigation.


{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#fig-diff-in-means >}}

## Research Design {#sec-design}

### Data and Experiment

Our analysis reuses the conjoint survey experiment of @hahm2024divided, an online study fielded across 25 European Union member states around the 2019 European Parliament elections.
The experiment embeds a randomized conjoint within two behavioral games.
In each task a respondent is endowed with ten tokens and decides how many to transfer to a fictional co-player whose profile is randomly generated.
The Dictator (or Solidarity) game records this transfer directly, whereas the Trust game adds announced reciprocity, as the experimenter triples the transferred amount and the co-player may return a share.
Each respondent completes three rounds of each game, for six decisions in total, with a fresh profile drawn for every round.

The co-player profile varies seven attributes: age, gender, social class, nationality, partisan affiliation, EU identity, and religion.
Both the order in which the attributes appear and the level shown for each are randomized by design.[^example]
Because some attribute combinations are implausible, @hahm2024divided restrict which attributes are displayed through four conditions that vary whether EU identity and partisan affiliation appear and whether the co-player is a conational.[^treatments]
For our analysis, we collapse partisan affiliation into a display-aware indicator of whether party was shown, not shown but the co-player was still a conational, or not applicable because no conational was displayed.
This lets us recover the average effect of merely displaying a partisan affiliation on the Muslim penalty rather than the effect of any particular party.
The religion attribute, with the four levels Muslim, Catholic, Protestant, and non-religious, is shown across all four conditions, so the full set of game decisions can be used to estimate the Muslim penalty.

[^example]: @fig-profile in the appendix shows an example profile.

[^treatments]: These randomly assigned conditions are: a conational with both EU identity and partisan affiliation, a conational with party but not EU identity, an EU national with EU identity but not party, and a co-player of some nationality with neither.
Partisan affiliation is displayed only for conationals, since assigning a national party to a co-player ineligible to vote for it would be implausible.

The design contains no condition in which religion is absent, so there is no pure no-religion control.
We do not adopt non-religious as the baseline, because being non-religious is not obviously a neutral reference and may be read differently across contexts, for instance in the post-communist states of eastern Europe.
To avoid the dependence of conjoint estimates on a chosen reference category [see @leeper2020measuring] and to simplify the causal language, we collapse religion into a binary treatment $T$, equal to 1 when the co-player is displayed as Muslim and 0 otherwise.[^binary-coding]
This coding follows from our interest in the drivers of the Muslim penalty rather than in the full multidimensional conjoint.

After standard quality filtering on attention checks and completed games, the analysis sample comprises roughly 28,200 respondents across the 25 countries, yielding about 84,600 decisions per game.
These data are inherently hierarchical: decisions (rounds) are nested within respondents, who are in turn nested within countries.
We include every covariate measured before the games and exclude those recorded afterward, to avoid post-treatment and collider bias.
The retained set ranges from demographics to attitudinal batteries on national and European attachment, populist, democratic, and technocratic dispositions, and evaluations of the European Union.
We make two exceptions for stable demographics drawn from the post-game block, age at which education stopped and residence type, which the experiment cannot plausibly have altered.
Appendix @tbl-wordings gives the full set with exact question wordings and scales.


[^binary-coding]: This coding effectively averages the non-Muslim religion features against the Muslim one. As we are interested in the drivers of Muslim bias rather than in particular facets of that bias, we adopt this simpler approach.


### Causal Estimand

While our analysis builds on the conjoint survey experiment fielded by
[@hahm2023divided; @hahm2024divided], our research question differs from theirs.
Original study focuses on the identity side of the story and how six different attributes of it, including religion, affect token allocations.
Since we focus exclusively on the Muslim penalty and ask what drives variation in it across individuals and contexts, it also changes the quantities of interest.
The conventional estimand in conjoint experiments is the Average Marginal Component Effect [AMCE, for definition see @hainmueller2014causal].
However, since it by definition is the average, it conceals the heterogeneity in how different respondents respond to the same treatment, which is exactly what we are interested in. Therefore, following @robinson2024detect, we adopt the idea of nested causal quantities.

Firstly, as discussed above, we simplify the religion attribute.
After this simplification, our treatment variable is binary: $T_{ric} \in \{0,1\}$, where $T_{ric} = 1$ if the profile is displayed as Muslim and 0 otherwise.
This change shifts our estimand from the AMCE framework to a standard average treatment effect logic.
We observe only the realized outcome, which can be represented in potential outcomes notation as following:

$$
Y_{ric}^{g,\text{obs}} =
T_{ric} \cdot Y_{ric}^{g}(1) + (1 - T_{ric}) \cdot Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
$$ {#eq-observed}

Since both games reflect different types of discrimination, we estimate separate models for each game $g \in \{D, TR\}$, where $D$ denotes the dictator game and $TR$ the trust game. Our baseline estimand for each game is the Average Treatment Effect (ATE):

$$
\text{ATE}^{g} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\right]
$${#eq-ate}

\noindent where $Y_{ric}^{g}(t)$ is the number of tokens allocated by respondent $i$ in country $c$ during round $r$ of game $g$ under treatment assignment $t$.
This quantity captures the average Muslim penalty in the target population. Since the religion attribute is randomly assigned by design, $\{Y_{ric}^{g}(1),\, Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\}  \perp T_{ric}$.[^balance]
However, as the sample was recruited to be representative of national populations only in terms of age and gender [@hahm2024divided, p. 73], we adopt the more conservative Sample ATE (SATE), which conditions on the observed sample rather than making broader claims about the target population:[^sate-choice]

$$
    \text{SATE}^{g} =
        \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i}
        \mathbb{E}_{r}\!\left[Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\right]
$${#eq-sate}

As we are interested in factors that explain the heterogeneity in  Muslim penalty,  we decompose the SATE into its conditional counterpart, the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE):

$$
    \tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric}) =
        \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            X_{ic},\, A_{ric}
        \right]
$${#eq-cate1}

\noindent where $X_{ic}$ denotes time-invariant respondent-level covariates, which includes HERE NEED TO ADD WHICH PRE-TREATMENT VARIABLES WE USE, and $A_{ric}$ denotes the remaining profile attributes displayed in round $r$, namely gender, age, social class, party affiliation and nationality.
Importantly, we do not treat these profile attributes as separate treatments as in the AMCE approach, but as potential moderators of the Muslim penalty.

This single CATE function captures two different sources of heterogeneity.
First, we are interested in *causal moderation*, namely whether and how respondent-level characteristics shape the size of the Muslim penalty.
Disaggregating to the individual level by averaging over rounds, we define the respondent-level CATE:

$$
    \tau_{ic}^{g}
        = \mathbb{E}_{r}\!\left[\tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric})\right]
        = \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            X_{ic}
          \right]
$${#eq-cate-respondent}

Second, we are interested in *causal interaction*: whether other profile attributes change the Muslim penalty.
Disaggregating to the profile level by averaging over respondents, we define the profile-level CATE:[^subscripts]

$$
    \tau_{ric}^{g}
        = \mathbb{E}_{i}\!\left[\tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric})\right]
        = \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            A_{ric}
          \right]
$${#eq-cate-profile}

These quantities are linked through the law of iterated expectations, which ensures internal consistency across levels of the hierarchy: $\text{SATE}^{g} = \mathbb{E}_{i}\!\left[\mathbb{E}_{r}[\tau^{g}(X_{ic}, A_{ric})]\right] = \mathbb{E}_{i}[\tau_{ic}^{g}]$.

[^balance]: @tbl-balance-round-mods and @tbl-balance-resp-mods in the appendix report the profile-level attributes and respondent-level covariates by treatment status. Both sets are balanced across Muslim and non-Muslim profiles, confirming that random assignment of the religion attribute holds in the realized sample.

[^sate-choice]: Since we are interested in treatment effect heterogeneity and not the average penalty itself, the choice between ATE and SATE is inconsequential for the main analysis.

[^subscripts]:The subscripts $r, i, c$ reflect the hierarchical structure of the outcome $Y_{ric}^g$ (round $r$, nested in respondent $i$, nested in country $c$) and not the conditioning set in the expectation. We apply the same convention to the covariates and the disaggregated CATEs: $X_{ic}$ carries the country index because respondent $i$ is nested in country $c$ (so $c$ is determined by $i$), even though country is not part of the conditioning set. Likewise, $\tau_{ric}^g$ retains $i$ after marginalizing over respondents. After marginalizing over respondents, only $A_{ric}$ remains as the conditioning argument. After marginalizing over rounds, only $X_{ic}$ remains.


### Estimation Strategy

Estimating how a treatment effect varies across units is a different problem from predicting an outcome, and a harder one.
The signal of interest, the variation in the effect, is usually far weaker than the variation in the outcome itself.
A method tuned to predict the outcome therefore spends most of its capacity on the prognostic structure that sets the level of the outcome, and little on the effect [@imai2013estimating].
The modern tools for this task grew out of regression trees and random forests, which partition the covariate space into regions and average within them, learning flexible response surfaces without a pre-specified functional form [@breiman2001random].
Two developments adapted this machinery to treatment effects, and they differ less in statistical philosophy than in where they place their corrections.
The first keeps the tree ensemble but disciplines it with a prior.
Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (BART) model the outcome as a regularised sum of trees [@chipman2006bayesian; @chipman2010bart], and were carried into causal inference by fitting the response surface and differencing predicted potential outcomes [@hill2011bayesian; @hill2020bayesian; @carnegie2019examining].
The second corrects the data instead of the prior.
Causal trees and forests split the sample so that the partition is learned on one part and the effects estimated on another, a property their authors call honesty.
Under regularity conditions, honesty restores asymptotically valid confidence intervals on the recovered effects [@athey2019estimating; @wager2018estimation; @jawadekar2023practical].
The same sample-splitting logic underpins double machine learning, which combines it with partialling the nuisance structure out of treatment and outcome [@chernozhukov2018double; @ratkovic2023relaxing].
Generalised random forests sit in this tradition, extending honest, orthogonalised estimation to a broad class of estimands [@athey2019generalized], and have become a workhorse for heterogeneous-effect analysis in applied work and in political science [@davis2017using; @zheng2023estimating].
The two families have been compared directly in @ratkovic2021subgroup, which raises two concerns about the Bayesian, full-sample approach.
The first is that it does not model the treatment as a function of the covariates.
The second is that it uses the same data to fit the model and to estimate and test the effects.
The first concern bites mainly when assignment is not randomised.
When the propensity depends on covariates that also drive the outcome, a method that ignores it inherits the resulting confounding, and the split-sample forest is then markedly less biased and better covered.
Under randomised assignment with known propensities, as in our conjoint, that gap closes and the two families perform comparably on this axis.
The second concern, by contrast, survives randomisation.
Whenever the effect surface is learned from the data rather than fixed in advance, using the same data to discover and to test it invalidates the resulting intervals, a point that does not depend on how treatment was assigned.
Bayesian Causal Forests (BCF) answer the first concern by giving the treatment surface its own prior and its own regularisation, separate from the prognostic surface.
This guards against the regularisation-induced confounding that arises when a single prior shrinks both [@hahn2020bayesian], an idea since sharpened with shrinkage and sparsity refinements [@caron2022shrinkage; @caron2022estimating].
They have no analogue for the second concern.
Standard BCF, including the hierarchical variants we draw on, uses the full sample both to fit the trees and to form the posterior intervals, with no sample-splitting step [@hahn2020bayesian; @thal2024aggregate].
The honesty of the causal forest is exactly that missing device, and it is the concern our randomised design does not dissolve.

Our design follows from this comparison and from the structure of our data.
We observe each respondent over three conjoint rounds, and respondents are themselves grouped within 25 countries, so the observations are clustered at two levels.
Treating them as independent would understate our uncertainty.
The causal forest accommodates this structure directly.
We cluster the honest sampling and the variance estimation by respondent, which absorbs the within-respondent dependence across rounds, and we enter country as fixed effects in the covariate matrix, which absorbs between-country baseline variation [@athey2019generalized].
The Bayesian branch absorbs the same structure through random intercepts and partial pooling.
An active literature extends BCF to these hierarchical and longitudinal settings [@thal2024aggregate; @prevot2025hierarchical], and a working implementation already places a random intercept on the prognostic surface for clustered, multisite trials [@yeager2019national; @yeager2022synergistic].
That implementation carries only a single level of random intercepts, so we extended it to the two nested levels our data require, respondent within country, under the same partial-pooling logic.[^nested-extension]
We then checked both estimators on synthetic data built with the same two-level structure and a known, planted treatment surface.
This was a smoke test of the machinery, not a validation against realistic confounding.
Each estimator recovered the planted effects within sampling error, but the nested BCF did so only at small scale, and at full scale the nested fit proved computationally infeasible.[^nested-ri]
The causal forest recovered the same quantities at a fraction of the cost, using the respondent clusters and country fixed effects described above.
We therefore adopted the causal forest as our primary estimator, since it delivers the honest, cluster-robust intervals the comparison above favours and scales to the full sample.
We keep the BCF as a robustness check, fit with country random intercepts only.
Because it can no longer model the within-respondent dependence directly, we recover that uncertainty after the fact with a respondent cluster bootstrap.
The two fits therefore do not handle the clustered structure identically.
The forest builds the respondent dependence into estimation, whereas the BCF drops the respondent random effect and patches the uncertainty back in, so their agreement is reassuring but not two fully independent confirmations under the same assumptions.
Throughout, we treat the country and respondent variation as a nuisance to absorb rather than a quantity to interpret, in keeping with our interest in heterogeneity within the sample rather than comparison across countries.

[^nested-extension]: @yeager2019national fit their hierarchical BCF with the `multibart` package [@murray2021multibart], whose C++ sampler already accommodates an arbitrary random-effects design. Moving from a single level of random intercepts to nested intercepts therefore required only a minor adaptation of the high-level R helper that assembles that design, not a change to the sampler itself.

[^nested-ri]: The sampler forms the random-effects precision matrix densely and solves it by Cholesky decomposition at every Gibbs sweep, a cost that grows with the cube of the number of random-effect levels.
With both country and respondent intercepts that number runs into the tens of thousands, dominated by the respondent dummies, which puts the full nested fit beyond reach within the project window.
Restricting the random effects to countries reduces that block to a trivial $25 \times 25$ solve.

Before turning to heterogeneity, we confirm that the forest recovers the average effect.
The CF estimates a sample average treatment effect of $-0.5641$ (SE $0.0190$, 95% CI $[-0.6013, -0.5268]$) in the Dictator game and $-0.6419$ (SE $0.0205$, 95% CI $[-0.6820, -0.6018]$) in the Trust game.
Both reproduce the difference in means reported above (@fig-diff-in-means), a first sign that the forest behaves as it should before we ask it about heterogeneity.[^bcf-sate]
We then test whether that heterogeneity is real with the targeting operator characteristic curve (@fig-grf-combined-toc, appendix), whose area lies well above zero in both games.
This indicates that the variation in the Muslim penalty is targetable rather than noise, which motivates the moderator-by-moderator analysis that follows.

[^bcf-sate]: The BCF posterior means for the same quantity agree with these estimates.

We present the results in the visual grammar of @green2012modeling, plotting the estimated conditional effect as a function of each moderator [@woody2021model].
Each panel shows the average effect within a level or bin of one moderator, taken over the distribution of the remaining covariates, so it describes how the Muslim penalty varies with that moderator rather than a partial effect with everything else held fixed.
For categorical moderators we plot the point estimate and an honest 95% confidence interval at each level, drawn as a shape and a line segment.
For continuous moderators and rating scales we estimate the point and interval at each value, binning age and years of education into a pre-specified number of bins, and connect them into a line with a ribbon, which lets the forest reveal any non-linear trend.
We overlay the two games within a single panel by colour, so that any divergence between them reads directly as a game-specific difference in heterogeneity.

## Empirical Analysis {#sec-analysis}


{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-prof >}}

- Interpretations: 
    - overall, Muslim penalty remains quite stable with regards to other profile attributes
    - nationality: systematic difference between Dict and trust game for non-eu-national muslim profiles (stronger penalty in latter), point estimate in dict game lowest for "co-national", in dictator the latter surprisingly is systematically lower (i.e. bias is higher, systematic as the confidence intervals do not overlap) than for non-eu nationals, though this pattern is absent for the trust game outcomes.
    - EU identity: positive or negative attachement not different, in Dictator point estimates for both slighly higher (i.e. less penalty) compared to control condition (not displayed), but overlapping intervals and not in trust game
    - strongest effect is in partisanship: if a partisanship was shown, this reduces bias compared to when it was not shown (comparison refers to conationals only), this is robust across both games
    - profile's age show for both games a minor "drop" for age 30 regarding point estimates, but not systematic (overlapping intervals across all ages)
    - gender: no systematic difference between male and female muslim profiles
    - class: no difference between lower and middle class profiles, but Muslim profiles presented as upper class show receive reduced penalty
    - overall: point estimates of trust game most of the time more negative, but rarely suggest systematic difference 


{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-resp-1 >}}

- Interpretations:
    - Gender: males penaluze more than female respondents across both games, but gender gap only significant in Dictator game
    - Religion: strongest bias from catholic and protestant respondents, weakest bias (judged by point estimates) by orthodox respondents
    - class: dont-know respondents show smallest bias across both games (but also widest intervals), significant difference between solidarity and trust for upper middle class respondents, point estimates suggest slighly lower penalty for working class in dictator, but not systematic
    - Residence type: slightly larger penalty from respondents in rural areas compared to large city folks, difference more systematic in trust game, small/mid town in between
    - Attachement to country: seems negatively related, although hard to judge as very few denied attachement in the first place (hence the super wide intervals)
    - attachement to EU: almost linearly associated with reduction in Muslim bias
    - attachement to Europe: less clear than EU attachement, uncertainty bands allow weakly positive (dictator) or flat (trust) trend interpretation
    - Can understand EU politics: no indication of moderation
    - Officials talk, not act: more agreement, more bias across both games
    - Good vs evil: seems to be negative trend from 3 to 6 but it seems also to bounce off / turn flat toward end of scale, unlike pop_reps
    - Compromise is selling out: again clear negative trend, the more agreement, the larger the Muslim penalty
    - Compromise is important: if at all, more agreement associated with slightly less penalty, but overall strong levels of agreement in sample (hence few observations with <= 4)
    - Age: for younger respondents (<30 years) uncertainty intervals "scratch" zero (no Muslim bias) more often and for youngest age (18) the point estimates even surpass zero, the only instance where we observe that happen, then distance between upper bound and zero seems to increase, indicating a negative trend up to age 65, where the upper bounds seem to rise again. This might indicate a U shape relationship betwen age and Muslim penalty, but it might also mechanically arise from the smaller sample size for 65+ (and thus increased uncertainty bands)

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-resp-2 >}}

- Interpretations:
    - listen to other groups: same as "Compromise is important", maybe for the right/upper half of the scale even slighlty stronger positive trend
    - Leave decisions to experts: for values <=3 very few obs, in range 4 to 7 negative trend in trust game, less so in dictator
    - leaders above ordinary citizens: as previous item, at most small increase (trust more visible) in bias with higher agreement, very few observations <=3
    - parties do more harm: across both games, more agreement -> larger bias
    - ordinary people dont know: pretty close to "stable" effect here
    - EU should pursue long term goals: more agreement, less bias across both games, though slope flattens the from 5 to 7
    - Eu should heed the people: more agreement, more bias
    - EU effect on economy (x axis to be flipped): the more negative the evaluation, the higher the Muslim penalty
    - EU effeect on culture/identity: the more negative the evaluation, the larger Muslim penalty
    - EU effect on political status: again more negative evaluations more Muslim bias
    - better to abolish EU: higher agreement, larger Muslim penalty
    - satisfaction with EU: the reverse, higher agrement yields lower Muslim penalty 
    - Age left education: pretty flat trend

- Overall: Overall we find substantial heterogeneity across respondent subgroups, demographics and pre-treatment attitudes, but with very few exception these heterogeneities relate to the extend of anit-Muslim bias, not the presence of it. Even in those very few exceptions, it is only the upper uncertainty bounds that just scratch the zero line, no (single) moderator seems to credibly nullify, let alone reverse, the (negative) Muslim effect on token allocation. 
Strongest moderation heterogeneity is found across EU evaluations/attitudes, where the pattern in the data generally suggest that respondents against EU integration or with negative evaluations of the EU show systematically higher levels of anti-Muslim bias than those favoring / positively evaluating the EU and its effects.
Still, even in the pro-European sides of the scales, robust Muslim penalties persist, albeit at lower levels. 
This impression is supported by the variable importance plot in the appendix (@fig-grf-combined-vi), which identifies the mentioned EU items as 4 of the 5 most "important" variables.

## Robustness {#sec-robustness}

The results based on the Bayesian Causal Forest [@hahn2020bayesian] yield identical patterns for the conjoint profile level moderators (@fig-bcf-combined-prof in the appendix).

The same picture appears for the individual level covariates. 
Although uncertainty bounds appear smaller for small-N levels / values, possibly a consequence of the "lack" of "honest" sample splitting approach, the substantive trends described in the results section seem to replicate.
At most, the moderation effects of continuous survey items appear more "smooth" in the BCF estimates, but direction and trends remain -- Muslim penalty is overall robust, there appears a small gender gap (though less so in the trust game estimates), EU related items show strongest heterogeneity differences across scales, and the same  populism items as described above are associated with differing magnitudes of Muslim penalty.
The only notable disagreement appears in the recovered heterogeneity of respondents' age.
In the BCF results there is no "touching" the zero effect visible for younger respondents, neither does the trend approach zero in larger values of age as described in the lose u turn interpretation above.
Also BCF shows more stable differences between the games, but this too might partially be an artifact of the smaller uncertainty bounds. 

## Limitations {#sec-limits}

Only looked at within covariate moderating heterogeneity, not cross-covariate interactions.
Focused on Muslim penalty, implicitly assuming this is primary signal respondents perceive in experiment, this might not be the case in reality.
To avoid potential post-treatment biases, we only focuses on pre-treatment covariates (expection being years of education and residence type). 
This led us to exclude theoretically / potentially interesting variables such as general left right placement, EU integration placement, nativist attitudes and more which future endeaouvors might shed more light on, incorporating systematic ways to assess these as well. 
Also the conjoint setup is inherently an artificial setting, though it serves to isolate causal effects of individual attributes, whether and how these translate to more complex real world settings is another questions, as perceptions play a major role there (put differently, in reality, people do not walk around with clearly visible labels).


## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}



## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Appendix {.unnumbered}

### Appendix A Additional Sample Details {.unnumbered}

#### Examplary Profile From Conjoint Experiment {.unnumbered}

![Player 2 Profile Example. *Note:* Figure embedded from @hahm2024divided, Figure 1.](https://static.cambridge.org/binary/version/id/urn:cambridge.org:id:binary:20240123150926403-0273:S0003055423000266:S0003055423000266_fig1.png){#fig-profile}

#### Distribution of Outcome Variable {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#fig-outcome-distr >}}

#### Distribution of Profile-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-balance-round-mods >}}

#### Distribution of Respondent-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-balance-resp-mods >}}

#### Question Wordings and Scales {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-wordings >}}

### Appendix B Additional CF Figures {.unnumbered}

#### TOC / AUTOC Curve {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-toc >}}

<!-- #### Best Linear Projection {.unnumbered} -->

<!-- {{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-blp >}} -->

#### Variable Importance {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-vi >}}

### Appendix C Main Results from BCF Method {.unnumbered}

#### BCF Moderation by Profile-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-prof >}}

#### BCF Moderation by Respondent-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-resp-1 >}}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-resp-2 >}}
